content.assessment: As an explicit no-new-policy baseline, passive demographic contraction shrinks the aggregate size of the selective track without altering its selection mechanism, access criteria, or the maintainer-type asymmetries driving segregation; from this lens it represents an uncontrolled natural experiment whose distributional consequences depend entirely on where contraction falls.
opportunities: Could be leveraged to consolidate provision in ways that reduce, rather than worsen, geographic access disparities if paired with deliberate planning (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)
strengths: Provides an empirically grounded counterfactual against which the equity effects of active interventions can be measured
Documents an already-operating structural trend (cohort decline, application shifts) that constrains how much marginal effect active policy can add
threats: Passive contraction is likely to be geographically uneven, potentially worsening rural-urban access disparities even as aggregate selective-track size shrinks, compounding existing geographic clustering of selective schools in urban areas (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)

Roma segregation and church-sector selective growth could persist or worsen entirely unaddressed under a no-policy baseline (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
weaknesses: Does nothing to address the core inequity-per-seat mechanism: SES-skewed access to remaining selective seats persists unchanged (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
Does not touch Roma segregation, which is distinct from socioeconomic segregation and has been increasing structurally since 1989 (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
Does not address church-sector growth, which is documented as amplifying segregation and selection independent of demographic pressure (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
